title: Cash vs Wealth on Paper
subtitle: Can you actually pay your bills today?

hook.metaphor: Imagine you own a ₹5-crore house but have ₹200 in your wallet, and rent is due tomorrow. You're *rich* — and you're about to miss a payment. That gap between being wealthy and being able to *pay right now* is the whole of liquidity risk. Plenty of healthy-looking businesses have died not because they ran out of value, but because they ran out of cash at the wrong moment.
hook.scenario: A profitable company has a full order book and valuable assets, but its customers pay slowly and a big tax bill lands on Friday. It can't find the cash in time and defaults — despite being, on paper, perfectly healthy. What killed it?

learn: **Liquidity risk is the risk of not being able to meet your obligations as they fall due** — even when you're fundamentally solvent. It's a *timing and cash* problem, not a *value* problem. Our company wasn't broke; it was illiquid at the worst moment.

The crucial distinction is **solvency vs liquidity.** *Solvent* means your assets exceed your debts (you're worth something). *Liquid* means you have cash available *now* to pay what's due. You can be solvent but illiquid — rich on paper, unable to pay the rent — and that alone can sink you.

Why does it happen to healthy firms? Because **assets and obligations don't line up in time.** Money is tied up in things you can't instantly turn to cash (inventory, slow-paying invoices, buildings), while bills arrive on their own schedule. A mismatch in *timing* is enough.

Liquidity has a brutal personality: **it kills quickly.** Credit losses bleed slowly over months; a liquidity crisis can be fatal in *days* or even hours once confidence goes. This speed is what makes it uniquely dangerous (and why bank runs are so feared — a later level).

It's a top concern for regulators precisely because of that speed: banks must hold liquidity buffers under **Basel** rules (the LCR and NSFR you'll meet later), and **APRA**, **RBNZ**, and **RBI** all watch liquidity closely. Being valuable isn't enough — you must be *able to pay*.
bridge: So at work, you can separate two questions people often blur: *are we worth something?* (solvency) and *can we actually pay what's due, on time?* (liquidity). A business can ace the first and still die on the second. Watch the timing of cash in versus cash out — because, as many profitable firms have learned, you can be rich on paper and still go under for want of cash this Friday.

interactions.title: What killed the profitable firm?
interactions.prompt: A profitable company with valuable assets defaulted because it couldn't find cash for a sudden bill. The cause?

options.label: It was insolvent — it must have been worthless.
options.feedback: No — it was solvent and profitable, worth more than it owed. It died of a *cash timing* problem, not a value problem. That's liquidity, not solvency.

options.label: Liquidity risk — solvent on paper, but no cash available when needed.
options.feedback: Exactly. Value tied up in slow-paying invoices and assets couldn't become cash in time. Being rich on paper didn't pay Friday's bill — classic liquidity failure.

options.label: Market risk — prices moved against it.
options.feedback: No market price moved here. The problem was purely that cash wasn't available when an obligation fell due — liquidity risk.

interactions.title: Liquid or just valuable?
interactions.prompt: Sort each asset by how quickly it can become cash.

items.label: Government bonds you can sell instantly
Invoices customers will pay in 90 days

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Liquidity risk is owning a ₹5-crore house with ₹200 in your wallet when rent is due — you can be solvent and valuable yet still fail simply because you can't turn it into cash in time.”

checkpoint.q: Liquidity risk is the risk of…
options: Your assets being worth less than your debts
Not being able to meet obligations as they fall due, even if solvent
checkpoint.explain: It's a cash-and-timing problem — you can be solvent yet unable to pay right now.

checkpoint.q: The difference between solvency and liquidity is…
options: Solvency = assets exceed debts; liquidity = cash available to pay now
checkpoint.explain: You can be solvent (worth something) yet illiquid (no cash now) — and the second can still kill you.

checkpoint.q: Compared with credit losses, a liquidity crisis tends to…
options: Strike very fast — in days or even hours once confidence goes
checkpoint.explain: Liquidity kills quickly — its speed is exactly what makes it so dangerous.
